entries.caption: Hero Video
entries.prompt:
Aerial photograph slowly tracking across a massive heavy engineering
fabrication yard at golden hour. Camera positioned 60 meters above ground,
looking down at a 25-degree angle. The yard stretches 200+ meters in every
direction — open-air assembly areas where completed pressure vessels (7m
diameter × 25m long, steel grey with fresh primer) sit on multi-axle
hydraulic transporters ready for dispatch. Cathedral-scale fabrication halls
with open bay doors reveal active welding operations inside — arc welding
glow visible in the deep interior. A 200-tonne mobile crane positions a
dished head onto a horizontal vessel shell. Workers in orange hi-vis appear
as small figures against the equipment. Pipe racks, material staging areas,
and a rail siding with flatbed wagons visible at the yard perimeter.
Golden hour light catches the steel surfaces — warm amber on the vessel
shells, long shadows across the yard, industrial atmosphere with slight
heat haze. The SCALE is the story — this is a shipyard for land-based
equipment. Even industrial lighting deep inside the halls contrasts with
the golden exterior light.

entries.caption: USP Section Background
entries.prompt:
Extremely tall vertical panoramic photograph of the interior of a
cathedral-scale heavy fabrication hall. Shot from ground level looking
upward toward a 35-meter ceiling with steel truss structure, overhead
crane rails, and industrial lighting arrays. The composition reads as
a vertical journey: at ground level, the base of an enormous pressure
vessel (8m diameter) dominates the foreground — its steel wall curves
away from the camera showing weld seams, lifting lugs, and scaffolding
platforms. Mid-frame, workers on elevated platforms perform welding
operations — sparks cascade downward. Upper frame shows the overhead
crane system, ventilation ducting, and the industrial roof structure
with translucent skylight panels allowing diffused daylight. The image
has extreme vertical depth — a sense of looking up through a steel
cathedral. Cool fluorescent lighting dominates, with warm tungsten
task lights on the work platforms creating pools of warmer light.
Steel blues, concrete grey, with orange PPE on distant workers providing
the only color accent. Documentary, evidence-based, slightly desaturated.

entries.caption: Integrated Model Background
entries.prompt:
Wide-angle interior of a heavy fabrication workshop at night shift.
The scene is deliberately dark — deep navy shadows dominate, with
selective pools of tungsten work lighting illuminating specific work
zones. In the near foreground, a worker's gloved hands guide a
submerged-arc welding head along a longitudinal seam on a massive
vessel shell (visible weld pool glow, orange-white). In the middle
distance, another vessel section under overhead crane shows the
scale — scaffolding platforms with workers appear small. The far
background dissolves into atmospheric industrial haze and the glow
of additional workstations. The mood is focused, competent, late-shift
intensity. Deep shadows, warm point light sources, the blue-white of
arc welding contrasting with amber tungsten floods. This image says
"the work never stops." Shot at ISO 3200 equivalent — controlled
grain, available-light documentary feel.

entries.caption: CTA Background
entries.prompt:
Wide-angle photograph of a project review meeting in an engineering
office that overlooks the fabrication hall through a large interior
window. Five engineers in smart-casual attire stand around a large
table covered with GA drawings, calculation sheets, and open laptops
showing 3D vessel models. Through the window behind them, the fab
hall is visible — a massive vessel under construction with overhead
crane, establishing the direct connection between design and
production. Available office lighting: overhead fluorescent panels
plus the warm glow of laptop screens. The engineers are shown from
behind or in profile — no direct faces. Hard hats and safety glasses
rest on the table edge, ready for a floor visit. One engineer points
at a drawing detail. The image conveys COLLABORATION between design
and manufacturing teams — the physical proximity of office to shop
floor is the point. Cool toned, professional, documentary.

entries.caption: 3D Plant Modelling
entries.prompt:
Interior of an engineering design office — three workstations visible
in a row, each with dual 27-inch monitors displaying SmartPlant 3D
(SP3D) or AVEVA E3D plant models in full color. The nearest screen
shows a complete chemical plant model — piping runs in blue, vessels
in grey, structural steel in green, with a plan-view layout visible
on the second monitor. The engineer's hands are on keyboard and
mouse — close enough to see the interface elements but the person
is shown from behind. Desk has technical references, company notebook,
coffee cup. Other workstations in the background with different
project models visible. Cool office lighting with screen glow
providing the dominant illumination. The image proves DIGITAL
CAPABILITY — real engineering software, real project data, real
workflow. Slight shallow depth of field focuses on the nearest screen.

STYLE: Documentary industrial photography. Shot on medium format,
Kodak Portra 400 pushed one stop. Cool-toned, slightly desaturated —
steel blues, warm tungsten highlights, deep shadows. Available-light
only: overhead fluorescents, arc welding glow, natural skylight,
workshop tungsten spots. NOT studio-lit. Imperfect, documentary,
slight lens distortion, natural available lighting, real photography
— not CGI, not 3D render, not stock photography. Surface texture is
critical: steel grain, rubber surfaces, weld beads, machining marks,
bolt patterns. Rule of thirds composition with negative space.
People shown only as backs, silhouettes, gloved hands, or distant
figures — faces never visible. Full PPE when present (hard hats,
safety glasses, coveralls). NO lens flare, NO HDR look, NO
oversaturation, NO AI art tells (too-perfect symmetry, melted
fingers, impossible geometry). Equipment scale reference: Jasmino's pressure
vessels are 5–8m diameter, 15–50m long, 50–500+ tonnes. Fabrication
halls are cathedral-scale (30m+ ceiling). Always include human
figures near equipment as small references. Include cranes with
visible tonnage ratings (100T, 200T), multi-level scaffold platforms,
vehicles or forklifts for scale.

entries.caption: FEA Validation
entries.prompt:
Close-up of a large monitor displaying ANSYS finite element analysis
results — a pressure vessel model with stress contour plots in the
classic rainbow spectrum (blue for low stress through red for high
stress concentrations at nozzle-to-shell junctions). The mesh is
visible — fine hexahedral elements at the critical zones, coarser
away from discontinuities. A second monitor beside it shows the
corresponding hand calculation verification sheet in a spreadsheet.
The engineer's hand holds a stylus, pointing at a stress
concentration zone. Printed calculation sheets and ASME code books
visible on the desk. The image proves ANALYTICAL RIGOUR — FEA backed
by hand calculations, not just pretty color plots. Screen glow
illumination, engineering office ambient. Sharp focus on the
stress plot detail.

entries.caption: Design to Production

Split-focus composition showing the transition from design to
fabrication. Foreground: a desk with a printed GA drawing,
3D-printed vessel model (small scale, white), and a tablet

window): the actual full-scale vessel being fabricated — same
geometry as the model, now in real steel, 6 meters in diameter,
on welding rotators. Workers on scaffold platforms visible through
the window. The juxtaposition of the small model on the desk
with the enormous real vessel through the window tells the story

transition from sharp foreground to slightly softer background.

frame holding a markup pen, annotating a weld callout. The paper
has the slightly yellowed look of plotted engineering drawings,
with title blocks visible showing project numbers and revision

screen, pointing with a laser pointer. Hard copies of the model
output are spread on the table for markup. The image conveys
CROSS-DISCIPLINE INTEGRATION — one team, multiple specialties,

control valves with instrument bubbles, a reactor vessel with
internal baffles, and heat exchangers — standard ISA symbology in
black on white with red markup annotations. The engineer's hand
holds a mouse, cursor positioned on a control valve detail. A
printed process flow diagram (PFD) with mass balance tables is
pinned to a board beside the monitor. The image says "process
engineering" at a glance — P&ID symbology is immediately
recognizable to any plant engineer. Sharp focus on the diagram
detail, shallow depth of field softening the background.

entries.caption: Water Treatment
entries.prompt:
Wide-angle photograph of a water treatment plant that Jasmino
designed and supplied — an outdoor installation showing FRP
(fibre-reinforced plastic) filter vessels arranged in a battery,
connected by GRP piping with butterfly valves. The vessels are
cylindrical, 2–3 meters diameter, with dished heads and multiple
nozzle connections. Chemical dosing skids with small tanks and
metering pumps visible in the foreground. The plant sits on a
concrete plinth with cable trays and instrument tubing neatly
routed. A control panel enclosure is visible at the end. The
setting is an industrial or municipal site — chain-link perimeter
fence, parking area visible beyond. Overcast daylight, the FRP
vessels have a distinctive off-white/cream translucent quality
with visible glass fibre layup pattern. Evidence of TURNKEY
DELIVERY — complete, commissioned, operational.

entries.caption: Heavy Equipment Bay
entries.prompt:
Wide-angle interior of the main heavy fabrication bay at the Jasmino
India facility. Cathedral-scale space: 40+ meters wide, 150+ meters
deep, 35-meter ceiling with steel truss roof structure. Two enormous
vessels under simultaneous construction: one horizontal vessel (7m
diameter × 25m long) on welding rotators in the near bay, one
vertical vessel (5m diameter × 15m tall) being assembled in the
far bay with workers on multi-level spiral scaffold platforms.
Overhead cranes with "100T" and "200T" capacity markings visible
on the crane girders traverse the bays on rail systems. Dozens of
workers appear as small figures against the vessel walls. Clean,
organized concrete floor with painted walkways and material flow
paths marked in yellow. Even industrial lighting, deep atmospheric
perspective toward the far end. The image proves CAPACITY at a
scale that makes visitors recalibrate what "pressure vessel
manufacturing" means.

entries.caption: Certified Welding
entries.prompt:
Close-up action shot of submerged-arc welding (SAW) in progress on
a circumferential seam of a massive pressure vessel shell. The
welding head rides on a track-mounted carriage along the seam —
granular flux is visible ahead of the arc, with the solidified
flux/slag crust behind. The weld bead is wide (15–20mm) and
precisely deposited. The vessel shell curves away from the camera,
establishing the enormous diameter (6m+). A welder in full PPE —
leather apron, welding gauntlet gloves, auto-darkening helmet
flipped up — monitors the weld parameters on a digital readout
panel mounted on the welding carriage. Welding procedure
specification (WPS) sheet clipped to a nearby board. The image
shows PROCESS CONTROL — automated welding with qualified
procedures, not manual stick welding in a backyard shop. Warm
tungsten lighting, slight orange glow from the flux, industrial
documentary feel.

entries.caption: Non-Destructive Testing
entries.prompt:
An NDT technician performing radiographic testing (RT) on a weld
seam of a pressure vessel. The technician positions a radiation
source housing (gamma projector) on one side of a nozzle-to-shell
weld while a film cassette is strapped to the opposite side.
Radiation warning barriers (yellow chains with "RADIATION AREA"
signs) cordon off the zone. The vessel is huge — the technician's
head reaches only partway up the nozzle neck. A second NDE method
visible in the background: another inspector performing magnetic
particle testing (MT) — visible black ink particles on a white
contrast paint surface around a weld, with a yoke electromagnet
in hand. The scene communicates QUALITY ASSURANCE — multiple
inspection methods, proper safety protocols, qualified technicians.
Cool fluorescent workshop lighting, documentary framing.

entries.caption: Blast & Prime
entries.prompt:
Interior of a large abrasive blast cleaning chamber — the space
is big enough to hold a complete pressure vessel (visible: a
horizontal vessel 5m diameter × 15m long on support cradles).
The vessel surface has been blasted to near-white metal finish
(Sa 2.5 per ISO 8501-1) — the steel has a uniform silvery-grey
profile with no mill scale, rust, or coating residue. A blast
operator in full protective suit (blast hood with air supply,
leather apron) holds a blast nozzle — the scene is captured
between blasting passes, showing the clean metal result. Spent
abrasive (angular steel grit) covers the chamber floor. Lighting
is harsh industrial — multiple high-bay floods illuminate the
vessel surface evenly for inspection. The image shows SURFACE
PREPARATION — the critical step between fabrication and coating/
lining that determines adhesion quality.

entries.caption: Final Inspection
entries.prompt:
A QC inspector performing final dimensional and NDE inspection on
a completed pressure vessel before dispatch. The inspector stands
on an elevated scaffold platform at mid-height of a vertical vessel,
using a calibrated tape measure against a nozzle flange face while
recording data on a tablet. The vessel is complete: all nozzles
welded, name plate attached, hydro test completed (visible
calibration stickers on pressure gauges still attached). Below,
another inspector reviews paperwork on a steel table — the
Manufacturer's Data Report (MDR) binder with ASME U-stamp
documentation. The vessel surface is in prime-coated condition
(grey inorganic zinc silicate). The image communicates FINAL QC —
the last gate before the vessel leaves the facility. Professional,
meticulous, systematic. Even workshop lighting.

identification tags. The calender itself is an imposing machine —
8 meters long, 3 meters tall, precision-ground rolls that cost
more than most vehicles. PRODUCTION SCALE — continuous process,
consistent quality, volume capability.

A compression moulding press in operation — the press (250-tonne
capacity, heated platens) is partially open, showing a multi-cavity
mould for rubber parts. Freshly moulded parts — rubber valve
diaphragms with precise geometry, flash still attached — sit on

chrome-plated and mirror-finished. An operator uses a brass tool
to extract a finished part from the mould while wearing heat-
resistant gloves. Technical drawings of the moulded parts pinned

entries.caption: Hero (800×533)
entries.prompt:
A rubber lining technician inside a large chemical reactor vessel,
hand-applying a strip of black natural rubber lining sheet against
the curved interior wall. Shot from the manway opening looking
inward — the technician is mid-application, pressing the rubber
with a stitcher roller to ensure full adhesion to the primed steel
substrate. The vessel interior is partially lined: the lower
hemisphere shows completed black rubber coverage, the upper area
shows bare blast-cleaned steel with adhesive primer (red-brown).
Portable work lights create harsh directional lighting with deep
shadows on the curved surfaces. The technician wears full PPE —
respirator, coveralls, gloves. Tools laid out on a clean sheet:
stitcher rollers, trimming knives, adhesive pot, measurement tape.
CRAFTSMANSHIP in an extreme environment — confined space, precision
bonding, zero tolerance for defects.

entries.caption: Evidence Strip (800×356)
entries.prompt:
Wide panoramic view of the rubber lining workshop at the HAW Germany
facility — a vast production hall with multiple vessels in various
stages of the lining process. Near foreground: a vessel on rotators
with the manway open, technician entering for interior work. Mid-
ground: a vessel in the autoclave (visible through the open end door
of the cylindrical autoclave, which is 6m diameter and 15m long).
Background: completed lined vessels on transport cradles, quality
inspection stations, rubber sheet cutting tables with patterns laid
out. The scale of the operation — multiple vessels simultaneously,
dedicated autoclave, systematic workflow — communicates INDUSTRIAL
LINING CAPABILITY, not small-shop patchwork.

IMPORTANT: Do not include any text, labels, watermarks, signage, lettering, or any written words anywhere in the image. Pure photographic content only.

entries.caption: Gallery 1: Hot Vulcanisation (600×450)
entries.prompt:
Close-up of the hot vulcanisation bonding process — a technician's
gloved hands pressing a freshly applied natural rubber strip onto
the steel substrate inside a vessel. The rubber sheet (3mm thick,
black, with a slight surface tack from the bonding cement) is being
worked with a serrated stitcher roller to eliminate trapped air.
The adhesive system is visible: the steel surface has a red-brown
primer coat, a black tie-coat layer, and the rubber being applied
on top — a three-layer bonding system. The technician works
methodically along the overlap seam. A spirit level and pattern
template nearby ensure geometric accuracy. Detail-level shot — the
CRAFTSMANSHIP of rubber lining is in the hand-application quality.

entries.caption: Gallery 2: Spark Testing / Holiday Detection (600×450)
entries.prompt:
A QC inspector performing high-voltage holiday detection on a
completed rubber lining. The inspector holds a holiday detector —
a long probe with a conductive brush electrode — sweeping it
systematically across the glossy black vulcanised rubber surface
at 10kV/mm (test voltage displayed on the unit). The instrument's
alarm indicator light is green (no defects detected). The inspector
works in a grid pattern, with completed zones marked by chalk
lines. The rubber surface has the characteristic slight orange-peel
texture of properly vulcanised lining. If any pinhole or disbondment
exists, the current would arc through and trigger the alarm.
ZERO-DEFECT QUALITY — 100% of the lined surface tested electrically.

entries.caption: Gallery 3: FGD Absorber Interior (600×450)
entries.prompt:
Interior of a completed rubber-lined FGD (flue gas desulphurisation)
absorber tower — shot looking upward from the sump. The tower is
enormous: 12+ meters diameter, rising 30+ meters. Every interior
surface is lined with chlorosulfonated polyethylene (CSM) rubber —
the lining has a distinctive slightly greenish-black appearance with
excellent surface finish. Internal spray nozzle headers, distribution
trays, and mist eliminator supports are visible — all rubber-lined
or constructed from corrosion-resistant materials. The scale is
dizzying — workers on platforms at various levels appear small
against the tower walls. This is Jasmino's FLAGSHIP APPLICATION —
rubber lining at the largest scale, protecting a 600MW power plant's
critical emission control system.

entries.caption: Gallery 4: Abrasive Blast Cleaning (600×450)
entries.prompt:
Surface preparation in progress inside a large vessel — a blast
operator in full protective equipment (blast hood with air supply,
leather protective suit) directs a high-pressure abrasive blast
nozzle at the steel interior surface. The blast stream is visible —
angular steel grit impacting the surface, removing mill scale, rust,
and old coating. The area already blasted shows bright, white-metal
finish (Sa 2.5). The unblasted area beyond shows the contrast —
dark mill scale and surface contamination. Spent abrasive
accumulates on the vessel floor. The intensity of the operation —
noise, velocity, protective equipment — communicates that surface
preparation is SERIOUS WORK that determines lining performance.

entries.caption: Gallery 5: Autoclave Vulcanisation (600×450)
entries.prompt:
A large autoclave (cylindrical pressure vessel, 6m diameter × 15m
long, horizontal orientation) with its end door swung open, revealing
a rubber-lined vessel being loaded inside for vulcanisation curing.
The lined vessel sits on a rail-mounted trolley that rolls into the
autoclave. Steam hoses and thermocouple cables are connected to the
vessel for temperature monitoring during the cure cycle (140–160°C
at 3–5 bar pressure). The autoclave control panel nearby shows
temperature and pressure set-points. The scale of the autoclave
itself — large enough to drive a small truck into — communicates
the INDUSTRIAL SCALE of the curing operation. Steel, steam,
precision temperature control.

entries.caption: CTA Background (1800×1000)
entries.prompt:
Dark atmospheric photograph of the rubber lining workshop at end
of shift — overhead lights dimmed, work lights at individual
stations creating pools of warm tungsten illumination. Silhouettes
of lined vessels on cradles, the autoclave end door visible in
deep background. A single technician's figure walking between
vessels, hard hat and tools in hand. The mood is INDUSTRIAL NIGHT
SHIFT — competent, continuous, the work goes on. Navy shadows,
warm point lights, documentary.

entries.caption: Hero (800×533)
entries.prompt:
Engineering office environment — an engineer at a large workstation
with three monitors displaying different aspects of a process design
project. The primary monitor shows a P&ID (piping and instrumentation
diagram) under development — standard ISA symbology, process flow
lines, instrument bubbles, control valves. The second monitor shows
a heat and mass balance spreadsheet. The third shows process
simulation software (PRO/II or HYSYS equivalent) with a flowsheet
and converged results. The engineer is shown from behind — focused
on the P&ID, mouse cursor on a control valve symbol. Reference
materials visible: process design standards (API 520, API 521 for
relief valve sizing), client specification document with markup tabs.
PROCESS ENGINEERING — the intellectual foundation of every project.

entries.caption: Evidence Strip (800×356)
entries.prompt:
Wide panoramic of the engineering department — a long open-plan
office with 20+ workstations, each running plant design software.
The scale communicates TEAM DEPTH — this is not a three-person
consultancy but a full engineering organization. Dual and triple
monitors at every station. Large-format plotters producing drawings
visible at the far end. A meeting cluster with whiteboards showing
process schematics. Engineers in discussion at one cluster.
Reference library shelving with engineering codes (ASME, API, IS).
Professional, well-equipped, systematic.

entries.caption: Gallery 1: Plant Model in SP3D (600×450)
entries.prompt:
A large monitor displaying a complete chemical plant model in
SmartPlant 3D — the model fills the screen in a birds-eye
perspective view. Visible: a reaction section with multiple vessels
connected by pipe racks at several levels. Structural steel in
green wireframe, piping color-coded by service (process blue,
utility green, steam red), vessels in grey solid. Equipment tags
and line numbers visible as annotations. The model represents
months of engineering — hundreds of equipment items, thousands
of pipe segments. A second monitor shows the same model area in
plan view for review. DIGITAL ENGINEERING at plant scale.

entries.caption: Gallery 2: Design-to-Shop Floor (600×450)
entries.prompt:
Split composition: a printed GA drawing of a pressure vessel
(rolled drawing, partially unfurled on a fabrication shop table)
alongside the actual vessel in early fabrication — the shell
sections tack-welded together on rotators. A fabrication
supervisor compares the drawing dimensions to the physical vessel
using a tape measure. The drawing title block shows "Jasmino
Corporation" with project and drawing numbers. The vessel in
fabrication matches the drawing — same nozzle arrangement, same
saddle support positions. SEAMLESS TRANSFER from engineering
output to manufacturing input.

IMPORTANT: Do not include any text, labels, watermarks, signage, lettering, or any written words anywhere in the image. Pure photographic content only.

entries.caption: Gallery 3: Client Design Review (600×450)
entries.prompt:
A virtual design review session — a conference room with a
projection screen showing a 3D plant model walkthrough (first-
person perspective through a pipe rack, as if walking through
the plant). Several engineers and client representatives seated
around the table with printed review packages, markup pens in
hand. The projected walkthrough shows an area where a piping
clash has been identified — red highlight on two interfering
elements. An engineer points to the clash on screen while
another makes notes. Laptops open with real-time model access.
CLASH DETECTION AND RESOLUTION — catching problems in the model
before they become expensive rework in the field.

IMPORTANT: Do not include any text, labels, watermarks, signage, lettering, or any written words anywhere in the image. Pure photographic content only.

entries.caption: Gallery 4: Piping & Instrumentation Development (600×450)
entries.prompt:
Detailed view of P&ID development — a large monitor showing a
section of a piping and instrumentation diagram being finalized.
The P&ID shows a distillation column with reflux drum, reboiler
circuit, overhead condenser, and product draw-offs — each with
proper instrument loops (temperature, pressure, flow, level
control). Instrument specification sheets open in a secondary
window. Process data sheets for the column and heat exchangers
visible on the desk. A red-line markup of the previous P&ID
revision lies beside the monitor, showing the evolution of the
design through client review cycles. DETAILED ENGINEERING —
the drawings that become the plant's operating bible.

entries.caption: Gallery 5: Commissioned Plant (600×450)
entries.prompt:
Aerial photograph of a completed and operational fertilizer complex
that was designed by the engineering team. The plant is running —
visible steam/vapor from process vents, vehicles moving on access
roads, control room lights on. The plant layout matches what a
3D model would show: clearly organized process units connected by
pipe racks, storage tank farms, cooling towers, utility areas.
The scale is substantial — the plant covers several hectares.
The photograph proves DELIVERY — engineering isn't just drawings,
it results in commissioned, operating plants. Late afternoon light,
slightly hazy industrial atmosphere.

entries.caption: CTA Background (1800×1000)
entries.prompt:
Engineering office at twilight — interior lights on, exterior
windows showing dusk sky. Workstations with glowing monitors
showing plant models and P&IDs. The atmosphere is focused,
professional, late-working-hours dedication. A few engineers
still at stations, shown as silhouettes against screen glow.
The office is modern, well-equipped — the kind of place where
serious engineering happens. Cool tones, screen glow, quiet
intensity.

entries.caption: Hero (800×533)
entries.prompt:
An equipment design engineer's workstation showing the core of
mechanical design work. Primary monitor: a 3D solid model of a
large pressure vessel in PV Elite or Compress — the vessel shown
in cross-section revealing internal components (baffles, tube
bundle, impingement plates). Dimension annotations, material
callouts, and weld joint references visible. Secondary monitor:
an ASME Section VIII Division 1 calculation sheet — UG-27 shell
thickness, UG-37 reinforcement calculations, with input parameters
and safety factors. The code book (ASME BPVC) sits open on the
desk. The engineer's hand holds a red pen, marking a design
review notation. STANDARDS-BASED DESIGN — every wall thickness,
every nozzle reinforcement, every weld joint designed to code.

entries.caption: Evidence Strip (800×356)
entries.prompt:
Wide view of the equipment design section — a dedicated area within
the engineering office where mechanical design engineers develop
pressure vessel, heat exchanger, and reactor designs. Each station
has specialized software: PV Elite, Compress, ANSYS for FEA,
AutoCAD for drafting. Large-format plotted GA drawings rolled and
filed in vertical plan racks. Physical reference shelf: ASME BPVC
volumes (the distinctive black and green covers), TEMA standards,
API code books. A 3D-printed vessel model (scale model, white)
sits on one desk for nozzle orientation review.

entries.caption: Gallery 1: ASME VIII Pressure Vessel (600×450)
entries.prompt:
A massive ASME Section VIII Division 1 pressure vessel nearing
completion in the fabrication hall. The vessel is vertical, 5m
diameter × 15m tall — workers on scaffold platforms at three
levels for scale. The vessel shell shows circumferential weld
seams (ground flush for RT examination), multiple nozzle
connections (man-way, process nozzles with raised-face flanges,
instrument connections). The ASME name plate and U-stamp
certification plate are being attached by an inspector. The
vessel is destined for a chemical plant in the Middle East —
the project is real, the engineering is proven. Cathedral-scale
fabrication hall in the background. ASME QUALITY at industrial scale.

entries.caption: Gallery 2: Shell & Tube Heat Exchanger (600×450)
entries.prompt:
A TEMA Type C (split-ring floating head) shell and tube heat
exchanger on the assembly floor. The exchanger is partially
assembled — the tube bundle is being inserted into the shell.
The bundle shows hundreds of tubes (19mm OD, expanded and welded
into the tubesheet) with segmental baffles visible at regular
spacing. The shell is horizontal on assembly stands, approximately
1.2m diameter × 6m long. A technician guides the bundle insertion
using an overhead hoist. The tubesheet face shows the precise
triangular pitch tube pattern (thousands of tube holes, each with
a tube expanded and seal-welded). PRECISION ASSEMBLY — the tube
bundle is a work of engineering art.

entries.caption: Gallery 3: Agitated Reactor (600×450)
entries.prompt:
A large agitated reactor vessel designed for a fertilizer plant
application. The reactor is vertical, 4m diameter × 8m tall, with
a top-mounted agitator drive assembly (heavy-duty gearbox and
motor). The vessel is shown in the final stages of fabrication —
the agitator shaft and impeller blades (anchor type) are being
lowered into the vessel through the top nozzle. The vessel
interior has been rubber-lined (visible through the open top
flange — black rubber lining covering the entire interior surface).
Baffles welded to the wall with rubber-covered tips. This vessel
demonstrates CROSS-DIVISION INTEGRATION — mechanical design,
fabrication, and rubber lining all by Jasmino.

entries.caption: Gallery 4: Absorption Column with FRP Internals (600×450)
entries.prompt:
A tall absorption column (3m diameter × 18m tall) with FRP internal
components visible during assembly. The column is vertical in the
fabrication hall — its upper section has been lifted off to allow
installation of FRP distribution trays, packing support grids, and
mist eliminators. The FRP components have the distinctive cream-
white translucent appearance of fiberglass construction. Workers
at height install a distribution tray — the tray's precisely
drilled orifice pattern is visible. The steel column shell with
its internal rubber lining (black) provides the backdrop. Multiple
engineering disciplines in ONE vessel: steel shell, rubber lining,
FRP internals, instrument connections.

entries.caption: Gallery 5: API 650 Storage Tank (600×450)
entries.prompt:
An API 650 welded steel storage tank under construction on-site —
a large cylindrical flat-bottom tank (approximately 20m diameter ×
12m tall, 5000m³ capacity). The tank is being erected using the
jacking method — the top shell course and roof are complete at
the top, with jack rods visible at the base lifting the structure
while a new shell course is welded beneath. Workers weld the
annular bottom plate in the foreground. The construction site is
in Southeast Asia — tropical vegetation visible beyond the tank
farm perimeter. Overhead crane and welding equipment staged around
the base. SITE CONSTRUCTION — tanks erected in the field to
API standards.

entries.caption: CTA Background (1800×1000)
entries.prompt:
The equipment design engineering section at a quiet moment —
monitors showing vessel models, code books on desks, the
fabrication hall visible through the window. Late afternoon
light entering from the window, screen glow from the monitors.
A contemplative, professional atmosphere — where precision
engineering begins. Cool tones, depth of field.

entries.caption: Hero (800×533)
entries.prompt:
An engineer's workstation devoted to 3D piping design and stress
analysis. The primary monitor displays a complex SP3D piping model
— a pipe rack section with multiple levels, color-coded services,
pipe supports, and expansion loops. The model shows high detail:
flange connections, valve handwheels, instrument tappings, pipe
shoes on support steel. The secondary monitor shows CAESAR II
stress analysis — a piping isometric with thermal displacement
vectors (arrows showing pipe movement), support loads tabulated,
and stress ratios at critical points. The engineer's hand works
the mouse, adjusting a pipe support position. PIPING ENGINEERING
— 3D layout and structural analysis in integrated workflow.

entries.caption: Evidence Strip (800×356)
entries.prompt:
Wide view of a drawing review table with piping isometric drawings
spread out — dozens of isometrics for a single project showing
the scale of piping scope. Each isometric shows a single pipe
segment with dimensions, weld numbers, materials, flange ratings,
and fabrication instructions. Rolled GA drawings of pipe rack
layouts visible beside the isometrics. An engineer marks up an
isometric with a red pen — checking a dimension against the 3D
model on a nearby laptop. THE VOLUME OF PIPING ENGINEERING —
hundreds of drawings per project, each precisely detailed.

entries.caption: Gallery 1: Full Plant Piping Model (600×450)
entries.prompt:
A large monitor displaying the complete piping model for a chemical
complex — the model in birds-eye perspective showing the full plant
scope. Multiple process units visible, connected by main pipe racks
running the length of the facility. The piping is dense and complex
— large-bore headers (24" and above) on the main racks, smaller
branch connections to individual equipment items. Color coding shows
different services: process (blue), cooling water (green), steam
(red), instrument air (yellow). The sheer density and organization
of the piping model communicates ENGINEERING DEPTH — thousands of
pipe segments, hundreds of support points, all clash-free.

entries.caption: Gallery 2: Pipe Spool Fabrication (600×450)
entries.prompt:
The pipe spool fabrication shop at the Jasmino India facility —
pipe spools being fabricated from 3D model-extracted isometric
drawings. A pipe fitter positions a pipe section in a welding
positioner, with the corresponding isometric drawing (generated
directly from the SP3D model) clipped to a board beside the
workstation. Cut pipe sections, elbows, flanges, and tees are
staged on a rack — each piece tagged with the spool number from
the drawing. A completed spool (pipe assembly with flanges,
welded and painted) sits on a transport trolley ready for QC
inspection. MODEL TO METAL — the 3D piping design flows directly
to fabrication without manual re-drawing.

entries.caption: Gallery 3: CAESAR II Stress Analysis (600×450)
entries.prompt:
Detailed view of a CAESAR II stress analysis model on screen —
a high-temperature steam piping system. The model shows the piping
isometric with node numbers, spring hanger locations (marked with
triangular support symbols), expansion loops, and anchor points.
A results table shows stress ratios at each node — color-coded
green for within limits, amber for approaching limits, with the
allowable stress per ASME B31.3 displayed. The load case being
reviewed is "Operating + Thermal" — showing how the pipe displaces
and stresses under combined pressure and temperature. The
engineer's notebook shows hand-calculated verification of a
critical stress point. STRUCTURAL INTEGRITY — proving that piping
systems will perform safely under all operating conditions.

entries.caption: Gallery 4: Installed Piping at Commissioning (600×450)
entries.prompt:
Photograph of a commissioned piping installation at a power plant
in Southeast Asia. The piping system is complete, insulated, and
in service — stainless steel identification tags on each line,
flow direction arrows painted on insulation jacketing. The pipe
rack stretches into the distance with multiple levels of services.
Close examination reveals the quality: consistent welding, proper
support spacing, expansion joints at designed locations, instrument
tappings precisely located. A commissioning engineer walks the
pipe rack on an access walkway, checking installations against
punch list documentation. THE FINISHED PRODUCT — design becomes
reality, operating safely.

entries.caption: Gallery 5: Auto-Extracted Isometrics (600×450)
entries.prompt:
A large-format plotter printing piping isometric drawings
auto-extracted from the SP3D model. The plotter feeds a continuous
stream of isometric drawings — each one a single pipe segment
with dimensions, weld numbers, BOM (bill of materials), and
fabrication notes. Freshly plotted isometrics are stacked on a
review table. An engineer picks up a printed isometric and compares
it to the 3D model on his monitor — a quality check before release
to fabrication. The volume of output is the story — one project
can generate 500–2000+ isometric drawings, each one auto-extracted
from the model. AUTOMATION AND ACCURACY — model-driven
documentation at production scale.

entries.caption: CTA Background (1800×1000)
entries.prompt:
Wide-angle photograph of a petrochemical plant pipe rack — dense,
multi-level piping stretching into perspective. Large-bore headers,
insulated hot lines with heat-tracing, instrument tubing runs,
cable trays alongside. Shot at dusk with the pipe rack lit by
plant operating lights — warm sodium-vapor glow on the insulated
pipes, cool twilight sky above. A few small figures walking the
access platforms. The pipe rack is the CIRCULATORY SYSTEM of the
plant — designed, fabricated, and installed from Jasmino's
engineering output.

entries.caption: Hero (800×533)
entries.prompt:
Wide-angle view of a water treatment plant designed and supplied by
Jasmino. The plant is operational — FRP filtration vessels (2m
diameter, vertical, cream-white FRP) arranged in a battery of 6
units connected by GRP piping with butterfly valves. Chemical
dosing skids with metering pumps and small FRP tanks visible in the
foreground. PLC control panel in a weather-protected enclosure. The
plant sits on a concrete plinth at a municipal or industrial site —
clean, organized, running. Overcast daylight illumination. TURNKEY
SOLUTION — designed, fabricated, installed, and commissioned.

entries.caption: Evidence Strip (800×356)
entries.prompt:
Panoramic view of multiple water treatment components in the
fabrication workshop — FRP vessels, GRP pipe sections, valve
assemblies, chemical dosing skids at various stages of assembly.
The workshop atmosphere is different from heavy steel fabrication —
cleaner, lighter, more precision assembly. Technicians assemble
pipe spools, test valve actuators, wire control panels. The range
of components shows the COMPLETE SCOPE — not just vessels, but
everything needed for a functioning water treatment system.

entries.caption: Gallery 1 (600×450)
entries.prompt:
Close-up of an FRP (fibre-reinforced plastic) pressure vessel for
water treatment — the vessel in the final stages of fabrication,
showing the hand-layup construction technique. The cream-white FRP
wall shows the glass fibre laminate pattern through the resin
matrix. Multiple nozzle connections with FRP flanges. The vessel
sits on a rotating mandrel in the composite workshop.

entries.caption: Gallery 2 (600×450)
entries.prompt:
A chemical dosing skid fully assembled and ready for testing — a
compact structural steel frame supporting metering pumps, small
FRP storage tanks for chemical reagents, piping with double-
contained sections for hazardous chemicals, and a local control
panel with PLC and HMI touch screen. The skid is complete, wired,
piped, and labeled — ready for factory acceptance test before
dispatch to site.

entries.caption: Gallery 3 (600×450)
entries.prompt:
FRP clarifier tanks (rectangular, open-top, 4m × 8m × 3m deep)
with internal weir plates and sludge collection mechanisms. The
tanks are in the workshop, nearing completion — the FRP surface
is smooth, joints visible at the wall-to-floor transitions. A
technician installs a weir plate at the outlet end. Adjacent
tanks at different stages of construction.

entries.caption: Gallery 4 (600×450)
entries.prompt:
Commissioning of a water treatment plant — Jasmino commissioning

technician adjusts a flow control valve while monitoring the PLC
screen showing process variables (flow rate, pH, turbidity). The
plant is running — water visible flowing through sight glasses,
pumps operating, instruments displaying live readings. The setting
is an industrial site.

entries.caption: Gallery 5 (600×450)
entries.prompt:
An installed and operating water treatment plant viewed from a
slightly elevated position — showing the complete layout: raw
water intake, chemical dosing, filtration vessels, clarification
tanks, treated water storage, and sludge handling. The plant is
in a tropical/semi-tropical setting. Well-maintained, operational,
with clean concrete surrounds and safety fencing. DELIVERED AND
OPERATING.

entries.caption: CTA Background (1800×1000)
entries.prompt:
A water treatment facility at dawn — soft golden light on the
FRP vessels and pipe work. The plant is running quietly — no
workers visible, but pumps humming, flow visible. The peaceful,
clean-water morning atmosphere contrasts with the industrial
intensity of other division images. Cool blues, soft gold accents.

entries.caption: Hero (800×533)
entries.prompt:
Close-up of active welding on a massive pressure vessel — submerged
arc welding (SAW) on a longitudinal seam. The welding carriage rides
along the seam, granular flux ahead of the arc, solidified slag crust
behind. The vessel shell curves away, establishing enormous diameter
(6m+). A welder monitors the digital parameter display. The weld pool
glow creates warm orange light on the surrounding steel. Sparks and
heat haze. FABRICATION IN ACTION — the core of what Jasmino does.

entries.caption: Evidence Strip (800×356)
entries.prompt:
Wide panoramic of the main steel fabrication hall — cathedral-scale
interior with multiple vessels under construction simultaneously.
Overhead cranes, welding stations, assembly areas, and material
staging zones all visible in a single sweeping view. Workers at
various tasks appear small against the equipment. The FACTORY AS
A MACHINE — organized, productive, capable.

entries.caption: Gallery 1: Vessel Fabrication (600×450)
entries.prompt:
A pressure vessel shell section being rolled on a plate bending
machine — the 400mm-diameter rolls bend 50mm-thick carbon steel
plate into a cylindrical shell course. The plate enters flat on
one side and emerges curved on the other. An operator monitors
the rolling process, checking curvature with a template. The scale
of the rolls and the thickness of the plate communicate the
HEAVY capability.

entries.caption: Gallery 2: Welding Operations (600×450)
entries.prompt:
Multiple welding processes visible in one frame: foreground shows
manual GTAW (TIG) welding on a nozzle-to-shell weld (the welder
in full PPE, welding torch and filler rod in hand, focused on the
root pass). Background shows automated SAW on a circumferential
seam of a large vessel. A welding procedure specification (WPS)
board with qualified procedures posted. PROCESS RANGE — manual
precision for critical joints, automated productivity for long seams.

entries.caption: Gallery 3: NDT (600×450)
entries.prompt:
Ultrasonic testing (UT) of a weld seam — a technician holds an
ultrasonic probe coupled to the steel surface with gel, scanning
along a circumferential weld. The UT instrument display shows the
A-scan trace — a clean baseline with no defect indications. The
technician marks tested zones with paint marker. Systematic,
documented, QUALITY VERIFICATION.

entries.caption: Gallery 4: Blast & Prime (600×450)
entries.prompt:
A vessel emerging from the blast chamber after abrasive cleaning.
The entire exterior surface is a uniform silvery-white metal finish
(Sa 2.5). A primer application crew stands ready with spray
equipment — inorganic zinc silicate primer (grey) will be applied
within the allowable time window to prevent flash rusting. Surface
profile gauges and environmental monitoring instruments (temperature,
humidity, dew point) are checked before coating starts. SURFACE
PREPARATION QUALITY — the hidden foundation of corrosion protection.

entries.caption: Gallery 5: Final Inspection (600×450)
entries.prompt:
A completed pressure vessel undergoing final inspection before
dispatch. QC inspectors check nameplate data, verify dimensional
compliance with calipers and tape measure, review the Manufacturer's
Data Report (MDR). The vessel is prime-coated, all nozzles flanged,
hydro test completed (test certificate visible on clipboard). The
ASME U-stamp nameplate prominently visible. THE GATE — every vessel
passes this final quality checkpoint.

entries.caption: CTA Background (1800×1000)
entries.prompt:
Atmospheric wide shot of the fabrication hall at shift change —
warm tungsten task lights on the work platforms, cooler overhead
ambient. Workers walking between stations. The scale of the
equipment dwarfs the people. A quiet moment of INDUSTRIAL
COMPETENCE. Deep perspective, documentary.

entries.caption: Hero (800×533)
entries.prompt:
An FRP vessel under construction — filament winding process. The
mandrel rotates while glass fibre roving, wetted with resin, winds
in a precise helical pattern. The machine's carriage traverses the
mandrel length, building up the laminate layer by layer. The wet
resin glistens under workshop lights. The resulting wall thickness
builds incrementally — a cross-section sample nearby shows the
laminate structure. COMPOSITE ENGINEERING — not fibreglass toys,
but pressure-rated process vessels.

entries.caption: Evidence Strip (800×356)
entries.prompt:
The FRP production facility — a clean, well-lit workshop with
mandrels, filament winding machines, hand-layup stations, and
curing areas. Completed FRP vessels in various sizes (from 1m to
4m diameter) staged for dispatch. Cut glass fibre fabric on
rolls, resin mixing stations, quality test coupon racks. A lighter,
cleaner industrial aesthetic than the steel halls.

entries.caption: 07 — Gallery 1–5 (600×450 each)
entries.prompt:
Hand-layup technique — a technician applies glass fibre mat
(chopped strand mat, white) to the interior of a mould, wetting
it with catalysed polyester resin using a brush and roller. The
laminate builds up layer by layer — each layer visible in the
cross-section at the trim edge. A consolidation roller removes
air bubbles. MANUAL SKILL in composite construction.

entries.caption: 07 — Gallery 1–5 (600×450 each)
entries.prompt:
Close-up of the filament winding machine in operation — the glass
fibre roving passes through a resin bath, over tension rollers,
and onto the rotating mandrel in a precise helical pattern. The
winding angle, band width, and tension are computer-controlled.
The mandrel already has several layers — the translucent cream
FRP wall is building thickness. AUTOMATED PRECISION.

entries.caption: 07 — Gallery 1–5 (600×450 each)
entries.prompt:
Assembly of a large FRP vessel — nozzle installation. FRP flanges
(hand-laminated, machined to ASME standard face finish) are being
bonded to nozzle necks that penetrate the vessel wall. An overlay
laminate reinforces the nozzle-to-shell junction. The workshop
shows multiple vessels at different assembly stages.

entries.caption: 07 — Gallery 1–5 (600×450 each)
entries.prompt:
Hydrostatic testing of a completed FRP vessel — the vessel is
filled with water and pressurised to 1.5× design pressure.
Calibrated test gauges visible. An inspector walks around the
vessel checking for leaks, delamination, or weeping. Test
certificate paperwork on a clipboard.

entries.caption: 07 — Gallery 1–5 (600×450 each)
entries.prompt:
FRP vessels and ductwork installed at a chemical plant — scrubbers,
tanks, and piping all in translucent cream-white FRP. The
installation is operational — chemical process running. The FRP
resists corrosion that would destroy steel in months. PERFORMANCE
IN SERVICE.

entries.caption: CTA Background (1800×1000)
entries.prompt:
The FRP workshop in soft light — mandrels, moulds, and completed
vessels creating organic curved shapes. The cream-white FRP
surfaces catch the light differently from steel — smoother, more
translucent, almost sculptural. A quieter, cleaner industrial
aesthetic. Soft daylight from skylights, warm workshop tones.

entries.caption: Hero (800×533)
entries.prompt:
A technician performing thermoplastic welding inside a vessel —
PVDF (polyvinylidene fluoride) lining. The bright white PVDF
sheets have been cut to pattern and positioned against the steel
substrate. The technician uses a hot-air welding gun with speed
nozzle to weld the overlap seam — the weld bead forms as a
consistent raised line. The stark white plastic against the dark
steel interior creates a high-contrast image. PRECISION PLASTIC
FABRICATION inside a confined space.

entries.caption: Evidence Strip (800×356)
entries.prompt:
Workshop view showing plastic-lined vessels at various stages.
Foreground: a vessel with PVDF lining partially installed — white
sheets pattern-cut and positioned. Background: completed plastic-
lined vessels with flanged connections. Thermoplastic welding
equipment, sheet cutting area, quality testing station visible.
SYSTEMATIC LINING PRODUCTION.

entries.caption: 07 — Gallery 1–5 (600×450 each)
entries.prompt:
Macro-close of a thermoplastic weld in progress — the hot-air
gun nozzle feeds welding rod into the V-groove joint between two
PVDF sheets. The rod softens and fuses with the parent material,
creating a homogeneous joint. Temperature indicator on the welding
gun shows the set point. The weld bead is smooth and consistent.
WELD QUALITY at the microscopic level.

entries.caption: 07 — Gallery 1–5 (600×450 each)
entries.prompt:
A loose-lined vessel interior — PP (polypropylene) loose liner
being installed. The liner is pre-fabricated as a complete
assembly that drops into the steel vessel with a controlled gap.
Spacer studs maintain the gap between liner and shell. The green
PP material fills the vessel interior. Installation rather than
bonding — a different technique from rubber lining.

entries.caption: 07 — Gallery 1–5 (600×450 each)
entries.prompt:
Holiday detection on a plastic-lined surface — the inspector
sweeps the electrode across the white PVDF surface at the
specified test voltage. Any through-liner defect would trigger the
alarm. The bright white surface makes the test visually clear —
the electrode trail is visible. DEFECT-FREE INSTALLATION verified
electrically.

entries.caption: 07 — Gallery 1–5 (600×450 each)
entries.prompt:
A plastic-lined vessel entering the autoclave for stress relief —
the lined vessel on a rail trolley is pushed into the large
cylindrical autoclave. Temperature and pressure will relieve
residual stresses in the thermoplastic welds. The autoclave end
door with its massive bolted closure is partially visible.

entries.caption: 07 — Gallery 1–5 (600×450 each)
entries.prompt:
A completed plastic-lined vessel — exterior steel shell with
primer paint, interior fully lined with bright white PVDF visible
through an open nozzle. The nozzle bore shows the lining
continuing through the nozzle and flanged at the face — complete
corrosion barrier from process fluid to atmosphere. Quality
inspection tags attached. DELIVERED PROTECTION.

entries.caption: CTA Background (1800×1000)
entries.prompt:
Workshop with plastic-lined vessels — the white PVDF and PP
interiors visible through open connections, contrasting with
the industrial grey exterior. Clean, precise, technical. Cool
workshop lighting, orderly layout.

entries.caption: Hero (800×533)
entries.prompt:
Spray application of an epoxy coating system inside a large tank —
the applicator in full air-fed respirator and protective suit
operates a plural-component spray gun. The coating deposits as a
smooth, glossy film — the tank wall shows the transition from bare
blast-cleaned steel (grey) to the first coat (amber epoxy) to the
finished system (dark blue/green vinyl ester topcoat). Multiple
coats visible in the build-up at the edge. ENGINEERED COATING
SYSTEMS — not paint, but chemical barrier engineering.

entries.caption: Evidence Strip (800×356)
entries.prompt:
The coating facility at GBT Germany (Marl) — a large spray bay
with extraction ventilation, environmental controls, and proper
containment. Vessels and tanks in various stages of coating — some
in surface preparation, others in application, others curing. The
facility is clean, organized, COMPLIANT with European HSE
regulations. Northern European industrial architecture.

entries.caption: 07 — Gallery 1–5 (600×450 each)
entries.prompt:
Abrasive blast cleaning of a tank interior in preparation for
coating — the blast operator works methodically across the surface.
The completed blast area shows near-white metal finish.
Environmental monitoring equipment (temperature, humidity, dew
point calculator) positioned nearby — coating cannot begin until
conditions are within specification. THE FOUNDATION — surface
preparation determines coating performance.

entries.caption: 07 — Gallery 1–5 (600×450 each)
entries.prompt:
Close-up of plural-component spray application — the gun tip
depositing a smooth, even film of vinyl ester resin coating. The
wet film thickness gauge (comb gauge) lies nearby for checking.
The coating colour is characteristic dark blue-green of high-
performance vinyl ester. Proper PPE, ventilation hoses visible.
APPLICATION TECHNIQUE.

entries.caption: 07 — Gallery 1–5 (600×450 each)
entries.prompt:
A coatings applicator performing brush application of an epoxy
system in areas that spray cannot reach — nozzle bores, bolt
holes, tight radii at weld seams. The brush technique is different
from spray: stippling into surface profile, building thickness in
multiple passes. The care required communicates that EVERY SURFACE
must be protected — no holiday, no thin spot, no missed area.

entries.caption: 07 — Gallery 1–5 (600×450 each)
entries.prompt:
Dry film thickness (DFT) measurement using a calibrated electronic
coating thickness gauge. The inspector positions the magnetic probe
on the coated surface — the gauge displays the reading in microns.
A grid of test points marked on the surface ensures systematic
coverage. DFT readings are recorded on a data sheet with acceptance
criteria noted. MEASUREMENT-BASED QUALITY — every square meter of
coating verified for thickness compliance.

entries.caption: 07 — Gallery 1–5 (600×450 each)
entries.prompt:
A large storage tank with completed internal coating system — the
tank interior visible through a manway opening shows a uniform,
glossy dark coating (vinyl ester) covering all surfaces. The
external surface is painted with identification markings. A QC
inspection tag and material certificate attached. The tank is ready
for service — decades of chemical resistance engineered into the
coating system. READY FOR SERVICE.

entries.caption: CTA Background (1800×1000)
entries.prompt:
The coating facility in late-afternoon light — spray bays with
extraction hoods, environmental controls, vessels being coated.
The organised, clean aesthetic of the GBT facility in Marl, Germany.
European industrial professionalism. Cool tones, systematic layout.

entries.caption: Hero (800×533)
entries.prompt:
A field inspection team performing condition assessment inside an
in-service chemical vessel during a scheduled plant turnaround. Two
inspectors inside the vessel: one uses an ultrasonic thickness gauge
on the rubber lining (measuring remaining thickness), the other
performs a visual inspection of a suspicious area using a high-
intensity inspection lamp and magnifying glass. The rubber lining
shows characteristic signs of service: slight discoloration, minor
surface roughness, but structurally functional. Inspection grid
markers (white chalk circles with numbers) cover the surface for
systematic documentation. The vessel is still installed — external
piping visible through the manway. EXPERT ASSESSMENT.

entries.caption: Evidence Strip (800×356)
entries.prompt:
Inspection equipment staged at a plant turnaround site — portable
cases containing ultrasonic thickness gauges, adhesion testers,
holiday detectors, Shore hardness meters, and inspection cameras.
Beside the equipment: inspection report templates, reference
standards (test blocks), and calibration certificates. An inspector
reviews a previous inspection report, comparing historical data.
The TOOLKIT of lining condition assessment.

entries.caption: 07 — Gallery 1–5 (600×450 each)
entries.prompt:
Close-up of ultrasonic thickness measurement on a rubber lining —
the inspector places the probe with coupling gel on the lining
surface. The gauge display shows the remaining lining thickness
in millimeters. The reading is recorded against the original
installed thickness to calculate wear rate. A grid of measurement
points ensures complete coverage of the vessel.

entries.caption: 07 — Gallery 1–5 (600×450 each)
entries.prompt:
A portable pull-off adhesion tester in action — a dolly bonded to
the lining surface is being pulled by the hydraulic tester. The
gauge shows the pull-off force at which the bond fails — the
failure mode (adhesive vs. cohesive) is as important as the
value. A dolly pulled off previously shows the rubber remains
on the dolly face — cohesive failure, indicating good bond
integrity. QUANTITATIVE ASSESSMENT.

IMPORTANT: Do not include any text, labels, watermarks, signage, lettering, or any written words anywhere in the image. Pure photographic content only.

entries.caption: 07 — Gallery 1–5 (600×450 each)
entries.prompt:
Detailed visual inspection of rubber lining — the inspector
examines a region showing chemical attack evidence. Under the
inspection lamp, the lining surface shows slight blistering
(chemical permeation through the outer layer). The inspector
measures the blister diameter and records the severity grade per
the inspection standard. Camera documentation of the finding.
SYSTEMATIC DEFECT RECORDING.

entries.caption: 07 — Gallery 1–5 (600×450 each)
entries.prompt:
A lining repair in progress — a damaged section of rubber lining
(approximately 400mm × 600mm) has been cut away, exposing corroded
steel beneath. The repair sequence: the steel has been blast-
cleaned to white metal, primer applied, and the technician is
now bonding a fresh rubber repair patch. The patch material,
adhesive system, and application technique match the original
installation specification. FULL-CYCLE SERVICE — inspect, diagnose,
repair, re-certify.

entries.caption: 07 — Gallery 1–5 (600×450 each)
entries.prompt:
A completed lining repair — the repair patch is vulcanised,
tested (holiday detection with passing result), and signed off.
The repair area is virtually indistinguishable from the
surrounding original lining. An inspection certificate and
repair documentation are attached to a clipboard. The vessel
is ready to return to service for another operational cycle.
LIFE EXTENSION — repairs that restore full protection.

entries.caption: CTA Background (1800×1000)
entries.prompt:
A chemical plant during turnaround — scaffolding around process
vessels, inspection teams at work, maintenance activities visible
across the plant. The controlled chaos of a planned outage where
every day counts. Dawn light, industrial atmosphere, urgency
balanced with systematic methodology.

entries.caption: Hero (800×533)
entries.prompt:
The rubber compound R&D laboratory — a scientist operates a
Monsanto rheometer, monitoring the cure curve on the instrument
display. The rheometer chamber is loaded with a test specimen —
the oscillating disc measures the crosslinking reaction in real
time. Surrounding instruments: Mooney viscometer, tensile tester
with specimens loaded, hardness measurement station. Sample
compounds of different types and colors on the bench with
formulation codes. Lab notebooks, reference standards,
calculation sheets. COMPOUND SCIENCE — systematic development,
rigorous testing, qualified results.

entries.caption: Evidence Strip (800×356)
entries.prompt:
Wide view of the laboratory and mixing facility together — the
R&D lab visible through a glass partition from the production
mixing area. The lab side shows instruments, specimens, and
formulation development. The production side shows the Banbury
mixer and two-roll mill. The physical proximity communicates
SCALE-UP capability — from lab formulation to production batch
in the same facility.

entries.caption: 07 — Gallery 1–5 (600×450 each)
entries.prompt:
Close-up of a rheometer display showing a cure curve — the
S-shaped curve of torque vs. time that characterizes rubber
vulcanisation kinetics. The curve shows key parameters: ts2
(scorch time), t90 (optimum cure time), MH (maximum torque).
The scientist's hand adjusts a parameter. Test conditions
displayed: temperature 160°C, frequency 1.67 Hz. THE DATA that
drives compound optimization.

entries.caption: 07 — Gallery 1–5 (600×450 each)
entries.prompt:
The Banbury internal mixer in operation — the large machine
(3m tall, 200L capacity) with the ram in the down position.
The operator at the control station monitors batch temperature
and mixing energy on digital displays. Raw material hoppers
with measured charges of carbon black, process oil, and
curatives ready for sequential addition. The power and scale
of the mixing equipment. PRODUCTION-SCALE COMPOUNDING.

entries.caption: 07 — Gallery 1–5 (600×450 each)
entries.prompt:
A bench display of rubber test specimens — tensile dumbbells
(die-cut to ASTM D412), hardness buttons (Shore A), compression
set specimens (cylindrical), tear test pieces (trouser specimens),
and aged specimens that have been through accelerated aging. Each
specimen is labeled with compound code, batch number, and test
type. The specimens show different compounds: some black (carbon
black filled), some tan (natural rubber), some brown (FKM).
QUALITY EVIDENCE — every batch tested to international standards.

IMPORTANT: Do not include any text, labels, watermarks, signage, lettering, or any written words anywhere in the image. Pure photographic content only.

entries.caption: 07 — Gallery 1–5 (600×450 each)
entries.prompt:
Freshly mixed rubber compound being sheeted on a two-roll mill —
the hot compound passes through the nip between the two chrome-
plated rolls, forming a continuous sheet 6mm thick. The operator
controls the nip gap and feeds the band. The sheet exits smooth
and consistent. A sample is cut for QC testing — thickness,
specific gravity, Mooney viscosity. FROM BATCH TO SHEET — the
intermediate form before further processing.

entries.caption: 07 — Gallery 1–5 (600×450 each)
entries.prompt:
The quality laboratory in full operation — multiple test stations
running simultaneously. A tensile tester pulls a specimen to
failure (the elongation display shows 450%). A chemical immersion
test rack holds specimens in labeled glass jars. An ozone chamber
(glass-fronted) exposes specimens to accelerated ozone aging.
Calibration records and standard reference materials visible.
COMPREHENSIVE QUALITY — testing for every property the end
application demands.

entries.caption: CTA Background (1800×1000)
entries.prompt:
The R&D laboratory at close of day — instruments quiet, specimens
staged for tomorrow's testing, formulation notebooks open. The
methodical, scientific atmosphere — rubber compounding is
chemistry, and this lab is where the formulations are born. Soft
overhead lighting, lab bench surfaces, orderly.

entries.caption: Hero (800×533)
entries.prompt:
A display of finished engineered rubber products — the range of
custom moulded and extruded parts that come from the compound
and manufacturing capability. Expansion joints, gaskets, valve
diaphragms, vibration mounts, rubber-lined pipe sections,
extruded profiles — arranged on a clean stainless steel table
with identification labels. The variety of shapes, sizes, and
rubber types communicates VERSATILITY — from 50mm precision
gaskets to 1200mm expansion joints. Well-lit, clean, catalog-
quality but still documentary.

entries.caption: Evidence Strip (800×356)
entries.prompt:
The moulding and extrusion production line — compression moulding
presses, injection moulding machines, extrusion lines with

IMPORTANT: Do not include any text, labels, watermarks, signage, lettering, or any written words anywhere in the image. Pure photographic content only.

entries.caption: 07 — Gallery 1–5 (600×450 each)
entries.prompt:
A rubber injection moulding machine in cycle — the injection
unit forces preheated rubber compound into a closed mould under
high pressure. The mould (multi-cavity, chrome-plated) is visible
in the open position between shots, showing the cavity geometry
that shapes the parts. Freshly moulded parts still in the mould
cavity. An operator extracts parts and loads the next shot.
HIGH-VOLUME PRECISION — consistent parts at production speed.

entries.caption: 07 — Gallery 1–5 (600×450 each)
entries.prompt:
A rubber extrusion line — the extruder head pushes rubber compound
through a precision die, producing a continuous profile. The
extrudate passes through a microwave vulcanisation oven (for
continuous cure) and then into a cooling trough. The finished
profile is cut to length at the end of the line. The die set is
custom — each profile requires a precision-machined steel die that
determines the cross-section geometry. Cross-sections of various
profiles displayed nearby.

entries.caption: 07 — Gallery 1–5 (600×450 each)
entries.prompt:
A product showroom/display — expansion joints of various types
(single sphere, double sphere, slip-type, hinged, gimbal), each
showing different rubber compounds and flanging options. Custom
gaskets in a range of sizes and profiles. Vibration isolation
mounts showing the metal-to-rubber bonding capability. Each
product category on its own display shelf with specification
cards. THE CATALOG — visual evidence of the product range.

entries.prompt:
Product testing — a large expansion joint being pressure tested
on a hydraulic test rig. The joint is installed between flanged
pipe sections, filled with water, and pressurised to 1.5× design
rating. Calibrated gauges, a technician monitoring for leaks, test

rubber mount on a cyclic loading rig, digital counter showing

IMPORTANT: Do not include any text, labels, watermarks, signage, lettering, or any written words anywhere in the image. Pure photographic content only.

entries.caption: 07 — Gallery 1–5 (600×450 each)
entries.prompt:
Finished rubber products being prepared for dispatch — expansion
joints wrapped in protective packaging, gaskets in labeled boxes,

The final step: QUALITY VERIFIED, DELIVERED WORLDWIDE.

entries.caption: CTA Background (1800×1000)
entries.prompt:
The rubber products manufacturing area — presses, extruders,
assembly stations — with finished products staged for dispatch.
The breadth of equipment and product range fills the frame. Warm

the products.

entries.caption: Chemical Processing
entries.prompt:
Wide-angle photograph of a chemical processing plant — a complex of
reactors, distillation columns, pipe racks, and storage tanks. The
plant is operational: visible steam from vents, process lights on,
vehicles moving. Dense piping connects the process units. The scale
is substantial — the tallest column is 30+ meters. Overcast
industrial sky, desaturated cool tones, documentary framing.
CHEMICAL INDUSTRY at operational scale.

entries.caption: Pharmaceuticals
entries.prompt:
Interior of a pharmaceutical-grade manufacturing area — clean,
well-organized space with stainless steel process vessels
(reactors, blending tanks) connected by polished stainless piping.
The vessels have mirror-finish interiors (visible through a sight
glass). Hygienic design: all connections are tri-clamp, surfaces
are smooth and cleanable. The area has controlled airflow,
temperature and humidity monitoring, and GMP documentation
visible. Clean, bright, PRECISE — pharma demands the highest
standards of fabrication quality.

entries.caption: Power Generation
entries.prompt:
A power plant with FGD (flue gas desulphurisation) system visible —
the absorber tower (large cylindrical vessel, 12m diameter, 30m
tall) alongside the main boiler house and stack. The FGD system
is Jasmino's core application: the absorber tower has rubber-lined
internals, FRP ductwork connects to the boiler. The plant is
running — flue gas entering the absorber, clean gas exiting
through the stack. Cooling towers and switchyard in the background.
POWER GENERATION — the industry that demands rubber-lined FGD
absorbers at the largest scale.

IMPORTANT: Do not include any text, labels, watermarks, signage, or lettering anywhere in the image. Pure photographic content only.

entries.caption: Fertilizers
entries.prompt:
A fertilizer manufacturing complex — acid storage tanks, reactors
for phosphoric acid production, and ammonia processing equipment.
The vessels are large and aggressively lined (rubber and plastic
linings visible where access covers are open). Pipe racks with
acid-resistant piping (FRP, lined steel). The setting is a large
industrial site in an emerging market. The corrosive nature of
fertilizer chemicals demands TOTAL CORROSION PROTECTION — rubber
linings, plastic linings, and specialty coatings throughout.

entries.caption: Mining & Metals
entries.prompt:
A mining operation's hydrometallurgical processing area — leach
tanks (large, open-top, rubber-lined steel tanks arranged in a
cascade), solvent extraction mixer-settlers, and electrowinning
cells. The leach solution has a characteristic blue-green color
(copper sulfate). The rubber linings visible inside the tanks
protect against highly aggressive acid leach solutions. The setting
is a semi-arid mining landscape — earth-toned, dusty, functional.
EXTREME CHEMISTRY — mining processes demand the most chemically
resistant linings.

entries.caption: Water & Wastewater
entries.prompt:
A water treatment plant — clarifiers (circular concrete basins
with rotating scraper mechanisms), filtration vessels (FRP,
cylindrical, arranged in batteries), and chemical dosing systems.
The plant is operational: water flowing, pumps running, level
gauges active. Well-maintained, clean surroundings. The plant
serves either a municipality or an industrial complex. FRP AND
WATER TREATMENT — Jasmino's design-to-delivery capability for
complete treatment systems.

entries.caption: Oil & Gas
entries.prompt:
A refinery distillation unit — a tall distillation column (40m+)
with associated heat exchangers, reboiler, overhead condenser,
and reflux drum. Dense piping with insulated hot lines. The
refinery setting: pipe racks, flare stack in the distance,
process fires in burners visible. The equipment operates at high
temperature and pressure — ASME-coded vessels, designed for
hydrocarbon service. Night shot with process lighting creating
dramatic industrial atmosphere.

entries.caption: Pulp & Paper
entries.prompt:
A pulp and paper mill — the bleach plant area where aggressive
chemicals (chlorine dioxide, sodium hydroxide) demand corrosion-
resistant equipment. Large FRP and rubber-lined vessels for bleach
towers, washers, and chemical preparation. FRP piping and ductwork
connects the process stages. The mill produces visible steam and
has the characteristic industrial scale of paper manufacturing —
continuous process, large equipment, high throughput. CORROSION
PROTECTION in one of the most chemically demanding industries.

entries.caption: Hero Fallback (1200×675)
entries.prompt:
Aerial photograph of the main Jasmino manufacturing campus at
Taloja, Maharashtra, India — approximately 80,000 m² of integrated
facilities. The campus layout visible from above: the main heavy
fabrication halls (cathedral-scale buildings with sawtooth roofing),
the engineering office block, the rubber products manufacturing area,
the composite (FRP) workshop, material storage yards, and the
dispatch area with completed equipment on transport trailers. Access
roads connect the buildings in a logical production flow layout.
Green landscaping along the campus perimeter. The surrounding
industrial zone of Taloja is partially visible — establishing the
location near Navi Mumbai. Late afternoon light, long shadows
emphasizing the building volumes. THE CAMPUS — the physical

entries.caption: Main Fabrication Hall (spans 2 columns)
entries.prompt:
Interior wide-angle of the main heavy fabrication hall — the
cathedral-scale space that defines Jasmino's manufacturing
capability. 40+ meters wide, 35-meter ceiling, multiple overhead
cranes with capacity markings visible. Two major vessels under
construction simultaneously: one horizontal (7m diameter) on
welding rotators, one vertical (5m diameter) on scaffold with
workers at multiple levels. The hall extends deep into the frame —
atmospheric perspective shows the depth. Clean concrete floor with
marked walkways, organized material staging. The SCALE makes the
first impression — this is not a workshop, it is an industrial
facility comparable to a shipyard.

entries.caption: Engineering / 3D Design Office
entries.prompt:
The engineering office — open-plan, 20+ workstations with dual/
triple monitors showing SP3D plant models, PV Elite vessel designs,
CAESAR II stress analysis. Engineers at work (shown from behind).
Large-format plotters, reference library, meeting areas with
whiteboards. Through the office window: the fabrication hall
visible, connecting design to production. Professional, well-
equipped, CAPABLE engineering organization.

entries.caption: Welding Station (SAW)
entries.prompt:
A dedicated submerged-arc welding (SAW) station — the welding
column and boom system with the automated welding head positioned
on a vessel circumferential seam. The welding parameters displayed
on the control panel: current, voltage, travel speed. The vessel
rotates on turning rolls while the welding head deposits the weld
bead under the flux blanket. Qualified WPS procedures posted on the
station board. AUTOMATED PRECISION — consistent, repeatable,
code-qualified welding.

entries.caption: QA/QC Metallurgy Lab
entries.prompt:
The metallurgy and QA/QC laboratory — a professional testing
facility with material testing equipment. Visible: a universal
testing machine (tensile/bend/impact testing), a pendulum impact
tester (Charpy), a metallurgical microscope with digital camera
for weld microstructure examination, a PMI (positive material
identification) spectrometer. Test specimens on the bench —
tensile coupons, Charpy notch bars, bend test strips. Calibration
certificates on the wall. The lab verifies MATERIAL AND WELD
QUALITY — the science behind the stamps.

entries.caption: Rubber Lining Bay (spans 2 columns)
entries.prompt:
Interior of the main rubber lining bay at HAW, Bockenem, Germany —
the world's largest rubber lining shop floor at 120,000 m². The
hall is enormous: multiple vessels in various stages of the lining
process visible in a single frame. Foreground: a vessel on rotators
with lining technicians working inside (visible through the open
manway). Mid-ground: vessels prepared for autoclave — wrapped in
heat blankets with thermocouple cables. Background: the autoclave
bay with multiple large autoclaves. The hall has German industrial
precision — clean, well-lit, systematic layout with clearly
marked zones. HAW heritage: 125+ years of rubber lining expertise
visible in the SCALE and CAPABILITY of the facility.

entries.caption: R&D Lab / Compound Testing
entries.prompt:
The HAW R&D laboratory — a modern, well-equipped rubber compound
testing facility. Rheometers, Mooney viscometers, tensile testers,
chemical resistance testing rigs, aging ovens. Test specimens from
multiple compound types — natural rubber, chloroprene, butyl, EPDM,
CSM. Formulation notebooks and digital data systems. The lab
develops and qualifies the lining compounds that have protected
critical industrial equipment for over a century. GERMAN PRECISION
in compound development.

entries.caption: Surface Prep / Blast Cleaning
entries.prompt:
The blast cleaning facility at HAW — a large enclosed blast chamber
sized for complete process vessels. A vessel inside the chamber
post-blast — the entire surface is white metal finish (Sa 2.5),
ready for lining application. The blast medium recovery system
(floor hoppers, elevator, separator, recyclable) visible.
Environmental controls — dust extraction, containment. German
regulatory compliance visible in safety signage and engineering
controls. SURFACE PREPARATION at European standards.

entries.caption: Curing / Autoclave Bay
entries.prompt:
The autoclave bay at HAW — multiple large autoclaves (6m diameter ×
15m+ long) for vulcanisation curing of rubber linings. One autoclave
has its end door open — a lined vessel on a rail trolley is being
loaded. Steam and compressed air supply piping runs along the bay.
Control panels for each autoclave show temperature and pressure
parameters. The scale of the autoclaves — each large enough to
contain a complete process vessel — communicates INDUSTRIAL CURING
CAPABILITY. German engineering, precision temperature control.

entries.caption: Coating Application Bay (spans 2 columns)
entries.prompt:
The main coating application bay at GBT Bücolit, Marl, Germany —
a purpose-built facility for applying high-performance coating
systems. The bay has full environmental control: temperature,
humidity, and ventilation monitored and regulated. A large tank
is being spray-coated with an epoxy system — the applicator in
full PPE works systematically across the surface. Extraction hoods
remove overspray. Adjacent bays visible with vessels at different
stages — blast cleaning, primer application, topcoat, curing.
The GBT facility is Germany's premier anti-corrosive lining
company — COATING ENGINEERING at the highest European standards.
Located in the Ruhr industrial region (Marl, North Rhine-Westphalia).

IMPORTANT: Do not include any text, labels, watermarks, signage, lettering, or any written words anywhere in the image. Pure photographic content only.

entries.caption: Workshop / Pipe Lining
entries.prompt:
The pipe lining workshop at GBT — a production area where pipe
sections are internally lined with rubber or plastic. Pipe sections
(300mm to 1200mm diameter, 3–6m lengths) are staged on racks in
various stages: prepared, being lined, completed. A technician
installs a PVDF liner inside a pipe section. Completed lined pipe
sections show the bright white plastic interior contrasting with
the steel exterior. PIPE LINING — a specialised application of
corrosion protection for piping systems.
